text: In this video, we are going to present the case of a fifty-six-year-old man who was in good health without any medical or surgical comorbidities that presented with a five-month history of alternating bowel habits, intermittent bleeding, tenesmus and weight loss. The initial workup showed an extensive mid-rectal mass with marked rectal thickening, but no distant metastasis. MRI staging showed a circumferential locally advanced mid-rectal tumor starting six centimeters from the anal verge invading adjacent organs and compromising the circumferential resection margins. Colonoscopy performed biopsies revealed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. The patient followed a treatment of neoadjuvant radiochemotherapy with a very good response, as shown here in his MRI and colonoscopy images. Given, however, the presence of extramural vascular invasion, the multidisciplinary meeting decided that this patient will have his quota of total chemotherapy up front.

The surgeon focuses on pelvic dissection, using a plaget for retraction to minimize trauma to the specimen. The dissection begins posteriorly, maintaining the embryological plane between the visceral mesorectal fascia and the presacral fascia. Monopolar diathermy is applied in a paintbrush-like manner at a low current to avoid presacral vein injury. Traction and counter-traction are utilized, with robotic arm articulation aiding the process. The surgeon carefully avoids going too deep to prevent exposure of presacral vessels, staying close to the specimen.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum on the lateral and anterior perirectal planes, performing a left lateral release to join the dissection planes. Care is taken to avoid injury to the left hypogastric nerve, which is identified and preserved. The dissection follows the curvature of the rectum, meeting the previously prepared posterior planes. The anterior tissues appear hardened, and the surgeon assesses whether the tumor extends to the seminal vesicles. After careful dissection, it is determined that the seminal vesicles can be spared.

The surgeon carefully progresses with the anterior dissection, ensuring not to penetrate the specimen perpendicularly. After crossing the Denon-Villiers fascia distal to the tumor, the rectal tube is prepared. Digital verification of the distal tumor margins is performed twice, followed by rectal washout. Two firings of the stapler are executed to secure the tissue.
Subsequent histology suggests extensive fibrosis after complete response to the neoadjuvant therapy.
